result:
The fan above my bed turns like a slow galaxy. It creaks once every forty-seven seconds, a grievance of bearings that only I notice because the nights here are so pearl-quiet. In this dark I keep a tally of its complaints, almost proud: forty-one clicks since midnight. I like the ordinariness of the habit; one needs patchworks of trivia when the heart is building cathedrals inside the ribcage but telling no one.

Memory smells are sneaking in through the grill of the machine: eucalyptus from the hillside my mother chased me down when I was seven and convinced the sky had stitched me into a secret; diesel from the first night train I ever boarded alone, seventeen and homesick, eating paneer sandwiches as Andhra Pradesh blurred past like an unrehearsed goodbye.

There is a reason insomnia feels like translation work. The brain keeps dragging the past tense into the present, keeps seeing the same words twice—with accent marks of longing, with umlauts of pain—until dawn acts like a proofreader who deletes everything in red. And yet the creak continues. Forty-eight. Forty-nine.

Some nights I promise the fan I will oil it tomorrow. I never do. The small tyranny of an unfulfilled promise is still sweeter than the tyranny of never promising at all. I learned that from my father who, when promised me a telescope, glued two toilet-paper rolls together, painted them midnight-blue, and called it a comet-catcher. I cried. But I still taped a slip of paper near the eyepiece: “Galaxies hereby requested to queue outside.” And I waited. Orion showed up; so did the roof’s dusty cat. That was enough.

Out the window the city is rimmed by sodium lamps, giving every rooftop a guttering halo. Below, a night food cart glows turmeric, one man flipping parathas like golden metaphors. People queue. Scarves, exhales, smudged neon. One woman in a mustard sari laughs so openly that the sound reaches me eleven floors up, wholly intact. I watch the cloud her breath makes and decide it counts as a miracle delivered at no fee.

I once wrote a poem comparing loneliness to an airport boarding queue. Airports are honest temples: they name departures aloud; they confess the wait. The poem was rejected by five magazines, each note saying it was too “trite.” I tucked the draft inside an old Vogmask box, spun the metal lid shut, and forgot. A year later my mother coughed blood into the bathroom sink. We rushed her to Apollo. While she slept under morphine, I sat in the linoleum hallway reading anonymous test-result printouts. On the reverse side of one was my poem, somehow photocopied. Maybe a nurse liked it, mistook it for scripture. I laughed so quietly the duty doctor asked if I needed sedation.

We buried my mother on a Tuesday wrapped in the mustard sari she wore the day laughter rose eleven floors, a sari she never actually owned. My sister insisted. Said the color was “a container she could grow into.” We cried like sea creatures learning footnotes of air. The funeral priest chanted addresses to pouring water, but my ears kept replaying the creak-thud rhythm of ceiling fans in every room we had ever lived in—each whispering unfinished maintenance chores—until grief itself sounded like poor ventilation.

The paratha vendor has switched off his wok. A final hiss, then quieter stars. I imagine the woman’s coin purse, still warm from her palm, resting now under a pillow beside someone who loves the soft clink of her bangles while asleep. In every city there is one heart instructing night how long it can remain casual before yielding to mercy.

Something moves on the ceiling: a gecko chasing the shadow of the fan. I root for the gecko. My sympathy is strategic; if fate is a game of hunger between creatures equally fragile, the smaller should win occasionally so that our dictionaries keep the word “hope.” The gecko makes its capture. I salute. Fifty-one creaks.

Tomorrow the building committee will email new water-cut timings: 6–9 a.m., 6–9 p.m. I will set more alarms than necessary. I will talk to the neighbor who wears ankle-length prayers, who once knocked at 3 a.m. asking to borrow printer paper so she could fold 108 paper boats and float her father’s medical report down the gutter in lieu of a Ganges he never reached. We bonded over that. I recall her eyes looked like incense smoke at the exact moment the stick tips into ash: still bright, but already flowing off.

Daylight will come wearing an orange vest, sweeping dust with veined hands. The municipal sweeper sings the same film song every dawn: “Chaudhvin ka chand ho.” He sings it wrong, but the moon never corrects him. I will open my curtains, and the fan will continue its circular autobiography above me. Perhaps this time I will actually oil it; perhaps this time the squeak will upgrade into a purr, a mechanical cat at last shedding its dialect of protest.

In the meantime, let me catalogue other small deferrals: the unread Sontag essay folded as a coaster under my tea glass, the stack of postcards addressed to no one in particular—India ink still wet as bloody apology—the donut plant drooping because I keep feeding it old chai instead of water. My chest stores entire museums of negligence, and yet each day the curator changes the lighting so that guilt transforms into something softer, almost antique.

An airplane crosses the sky, red eye blinking like punctuation deciding where the night ends. For passengers above, the city is a lava lamp of clues: gold here, violet there, somebody frying onions. Few will look down; even fewer will imagine me recumbent in this pool of weaving shadows, tracking a fan like a monk counts rosaries. It is the May of separations already. Airports will grow busier. Goodbyes will rust the air with salt. I will wave from balconies too far away to be seen, but I will wave anyway, pretending wind is a secondary language my wrists speak fluently.

The gecko completes its tour and rests upside-down directly over my head—its heart visible as a green strobe under translucent skin. We coexist under one imperfect sky appliance. Maybe the entire universe is just ceiling fans and geckos and night vendors and mothers wrapped in borrowed saffron, all of us whirring, coughing, flipping, folding, promising to do better, quietly forgiving ourselves by morning.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
